children: Saliva is essential for washing away food particles and bacteria. Reduced saliva flow allows bacteria to thrive, producing volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath.
Some medications release chemicals that are excreted through the lungs, leading to unpleasant breath.
Medications like antibiotics can disrupt the natural balance of bacteria in the mouth, leading to overgrowth of odor-causing bacteria.
Diuretics and other drugs can cause dehydration, reducing saliva production and contributing to bad breath.

data.value: Various diabetes-related issues can lead to bad breath including ketone bodies

Kidney diseases can lead to fishy or ammonia-like breath
GI disorders can result in bad breath from reflux or infections
Can produce foul-smelling breath
Conditions like Sjögren's can cause dry mouth and halitosis
Can lead to oral infections causing bad breath
Certain cancers and treatments can result in halitosis
Can cause distinctive bad breath
